risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "eligible patients were randomly assigned to receive either 12 mg OTO-104 or placebo on Day 1 using a 1:1 allocation ratio based on a computer-generated permuted block randomization algorithm." Comment: This describes a computer-generated randomization procedure with permuted blocks, which is a proper random allocation method and provides sufficient detail for Low risk.

Quote: "eligible patients were randomly assigned to receive either 12 mg OTO-104 or placebo on Day 1 using a 1:1 allocation ratio based on a computer-generated permuted block randomization algorithm." Comment: This describes the random sequence generation, but it does not state how allocation was concealed from the recruiters/enrolling investigators. No central randomization, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is explicitly reported, so foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The physician administering the study drug was blinded up to the point of injection, but differences in the opacity between placebo and OTO-104 prevented blinding beyond that point. The administering physicians were trained not to use video monitors or discuss the appearance of the injected materials that would unblind the study staff or patients." Comment: Although the study was described as double-blind, the report explicitly states that blinding could not be maintained after injection because the treatment and placebo differed in opacity. Since participants and personnel could plausibly become aware of allocation, and the main outcomes (vertigo diaries, tinnitus, quality of life, and some safety-related subjective assessments) could be influenced by knowledge of assignment, this represents high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "Patients recorded their daily vertigo and tinnitus experiences via an interactive voice response system diary during the 4-week lead-in period and 4-month follow-up period." "The physician administering the study drug was blinded up to the point of injection, but differences in the opacity between placebo and OTO-104 prevented blinding beyond that point." Comment: The main efficacy outcomes (vertigo rate, vertigo severity, tinnitus, SF-36) were patient self-reported, so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor; because participants were not blinded after injection, knowledge of allocation could influence reporting/measurement of these subjective outcomes. Although safety outcomes such as audiometry, laboratory tests, tympanometry, and word recognition are more objective, the primary and key secondary outcomes relevant to this study were subjective and therefore at high risk of detection bias.

Quote: "A total of 154 (77 in each treatment group) men and women participated in the trials"; "Patients completing study, n (%) 75 (97.4%)" ... "73 (94.8%)"; "No treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAE) resulted in patient discontinuation from this trial." Comment: Follow-up appears almost complete, with only 2/77 losses in the OTO-104 group and 4/77 in the placebo group (overall attrition about 3.9%), which is well within a low-risk range and not meaningfully imbalanced. Reasons for discontinuation are not described in detail here, but the paper explicitly states that no TEAE caused discontinuation and most participants completed the study, so incomplete outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
